instruction: What is the most played deck in Legacy right now?
output: Dimir Tempo is the most played deck in Legacy at 14.6% meta share with 228 decks in the current sample. It uses Orcish Bowmasters, Tamiyo Inquisitive Student, Murktide Regent, and Barrowgoyf as threats, backed by Brainstorm, Ponder, Force of Will, Daze, and Thoughtseize. Its win rate is approximately 52.7%, which is strong but not oppressive. The format is considered healthy with no single deck dominating.

instruction: What are the top 5 most played Legacy decks?
output: The top 5 Legacy decks by meta share are: 1) Dimir Tempo at 14.6% — the clear best deck, UB tempo with Bowmasters and Thoughtseize. 2) Ocelot Pride Midrange at 8.5% — non-blue aggro/midrange using MH3 energy cards. 3) Oops! All Spells at 8.2% — zero-land combo that mills itself and wins with Thassa's Oracle. 4) Sneak and Show at 6.7% — cheats Emrakul or Atraxa into play with Show and Tell or Sneak Attack. 5) Izzet Delver at 4.3% — UR tempo with Delver of Secrets, Lightning Bolt, and Murktide Regent.

instruction: Is combo or fair strategies more popular in Legacy?
output: Both are well-represented but combo holds a slight edge in total meta share. Combo archetypes collectively make up about 40% of the field — spread across Oops (8.2%), Sneak and Show (6.7%), Painter (4.1%), TES (3.5%), Mystic Forge (3.0%), Doomsday (2.7%), and others. Tempo and aggro combine for about 30%, control and prison about 20%, and midrange about 10%. However, Dimir Tempo (a fair deck) is the single most played deck at 14.6%. Combo is more diverse but no single combo deck dominates.

instruction: What deck should I play if I want to beat Dimir Tempo?
output: The best decks against Dimir Tempo are: Lands (3.8%) — its Wasteland-proof mana base, Maze of Ith, and Punishing Fire grind out Dimir Tempo's small threats, plus the Dark Depths combo threatens a sudden 20/20. Eldrazi (3.1%) — Chalice of the Void on 1 shuts off cantrips and Thought-Knot Seer strips their hand. Ocelot Pride Midrange (8.5%) — goes wide with tokens that overwhelm one-for-one removal. These decks exploit Dimir Tempo's weaknesses: it struggles against strategies that don't care about Daze, can't be disrupted by Thoughtseize, or go wider than its removal can handle.

instruction: Is Legacy a healthy format right now?
output: Yes. The March 2026 B&R announcement made no changes, confirming WotC considers the format healthy. The data supports this: no deck has a win rate consistently above 55% (the top deck, Dimir Tempo, sits at 52.7%), there are 54 distinct competitive archetypes across 6,433 deck entries and roughly 35,000 matches since the Entomb ban in November 2025. Combo, tempo, control, prison, and midrange are all viable. The format is less blue-dependent than any time in recent memory thanks to MH3 energy cards enabling non-blue aggro.

instruction: What is the Legacy metagame breakdown by strategy type?
output: The Legacy metagame breaks down roughly as: Combo ~40% (the most diverse category — includes Oops, Sneak and Show, Storm, Doomsday, Painter, Mystic Forge, Aluren, and more), Tempo/Aggro ~30% (dominated by Dimir Tempo at 14.6%, plus Izzet Delver, Mardu Energy), Control/Prison ~20% (Lands, Beanstalk Control, Red Stompy, Affinity Stompy, Death and Taxes), and Midrange ~10% (Ocelot Pride Midrange, Mono Black, Jund). No single macro-archetype dominates, which is a sign of format health.

instruction: What are the tier 1 decks in Legacy?
output: The current tier 1 Legacy decks are: Dimir Tempo (14.6%), Ocelot Pride Midrange (8.5%), Oops! All Spells (8.2%), and Sneak and Show (6.7%). Tier 2 includes Izzet Delver (4.3%), Painter (4.1%), Lands (3.8%), The EPIC Storm (3.5%), Eldrazi (3.1%), Mardu Energy (3.0%), and Mystic Forge Combo (3.0%). Tier 3 includes Doomsday (2.7%), Red Stompy (2.7%), Affinity (2.4%), Beanstalk Control (2.3%), and Death and Taxes (2.0%). The tiers are based on meta share and tournament results.

instruction: What decks have been winning Legacy tournaments recently?
output: Recent Legacy tournament results (April 2026) show strong diversity: the Legacy Showcase Qualifier was won by Sultai Aluren with Dimir Tempo, Sneak and Show, Lands, and Death and Taxes in the top 8. Legacy Challenge 32 events saw wins from Colorless and Oops! All Spells. League 5-0s include Cradle Control, Mystic Forge Combo, Azorius Stoneblade, and Mono-Green Cloudpost. The takeaway is that diverse strategies are winning — combo, tempo, control, and prison all appear in top 8s consistently.

instruction: How did the Entomb ban affect Legacy?
output: The Entomb ban in November 2025 removed BR Reanimator as a tier 1 strategy. Before the ban, Reanimator could Entomb a Griselbrand or Atraxa on turn 1 and reanimate it immediately. The deck still exists using Faithful Mending and other discard outlets, but it is significantly slower and less consistent. The meta share previously occupied by Reanimator was absorbed primarily by Oops! All Spells (now 8.2%) and Sneak and Show (6.7%) — other ways to cheat big creatures into play. Overall, the ban is considered healthy for the format.

instruction: What impact have MH3 energy cards had on Legacy?
output: Modern Horizons 3 energy cards have significantly changed Legacy by creating viable non-blue aggressive strategies. Ocelot Pride, Guide of Souls, Amped Raptor, and Ajani Nacatl Pariah form the core of Ocelot Pride Midrange (8.5% meta share) and Mardu Energy (3.0%). These decks don't need Brainstorm or Force of Will — they win through raw card quality and board presence. The impact is that Legacy is less 'blue or bust' than any time in recent memory. Non-blue aggro and midrange have real competitive teeth.

instruction: What are the win rates of the top Legacy decks?
output: Win rate data from the current Legacy meta: Oops! All Spells has the highest non-mirror win rate at approximately 53%, which is strong but considered fair by format standards. Dimir Tempo sits at 52.7% — the most popular deck but not oppressively high. Izzet Tempo underperforms relative to its play rate at around 49.8%. Overall, the format range is 49-53% for major archetypes, and a healthy format has no deck consistently above 55%. Legacy currently meets this criterion.

instruction: What are the most played cards in Legacy?
output: The most played cards in Legacy by percentage of decks: Wasteland (48%), Force of Will (45%), Brainstorm (43%), Thoughtseize (43%), Lotus Petal (41%), Ponder (38%), Leyline of the Void (36%), Force of Negation (35%), Orcish Bowmasters (29%), and Daze (27%). Wasteland being #1 reflects Legacy's emphasis on mana denial. The high rate of Leyline of the Void (36%) shows how much graveyard hate the format demands given combo diversity.

instruction: What is the Dimir Tempo vs Oops! All Spells matchup like?
output: Game 1 is even to slightly unfavorable for Dimir Tempo because Oops is faster and can win turn 1 before Thoughtseize can be cast. Force of Will and Daze help but Oops runs its own Force of Will and Pact of Negation for protection. Post-sideboard the matchup flips to favorable for Dimir Tempo with Leyline of the Void and Surgical Extraction. Oops is extremely vulnerable to graveyard hate since its entire combo goes through the graveyard (Narcomoeba triggers, Dread Return flashback).

instruction: What is the Dimir Tempo vs Lands matchup like?
output: This is unfavorable for Dimir Tempo. Lands has a Wasteland-proof mana base (most of its lands are basics or utility lands that don't care about Wasteland), Maze of Ith blanks creature attacks, and Punishing Fire plus Grove of the Burnwillows creates a recurring removal engine that grinds out small threats. The Dark Depths plus Thespian's Stage combo threatens a sudden 20/20 indestructible flying token. Dimir Tempo's disruption (Daze, Thoughtseize) is less effective against a land-based strategy.

instruction: Are artifact strategies good in Legacy right now?
output: Yes, artifact strategies are thriving. Collectively they represent about 15% of the field: Painter (4.1%), Mystic Forge Combo (3.0%), Affinity Stompy (2.4%), and various 8-Cast variants. Urza's Saga is the glue card enabling many of these — it creates construct tokens and tutors for artifact win conditions. Chalice of the Void on 1 remains one of the best cards against the cantrip-heavy metagame (43% of decks play Brainstorm). The Ancient Tomb and City of Traitors fast mana package lets these decks deploy threats before opponents can set up.

instruction: How much does a competitive Legacy deck cost?
output: Legacy deck costs vary widely. Budget options: Oops! All Spells ($1,102), Belcher ($1,200), Burn ($1,500) — these avoid dual lands entirely. Mid-range: Eldrazi ($2,128), Painter ($2,481), Ocelot Pride ($2,569). Expensive: Dimir Tempo ($4,955), Sneak and Show ($4,370), Doomsday ($5,285), TES ($5,657). Very expensive: Lands ($8,284, driven by The Tabernacle at Pendrell Vale at $5,000+), Colorless Stompy ($13,328). The overwhelming cost driver is Reserved List cards, especially dual lands like Underground Sea ($600-800 each).

instruction: What should I play in a combo-heavy Legacy meta?
output: In a combo-heavy meta, you want decks with free interaction: Dimir Tempo (14.6%) has Force of Will, Daze, and Thoughtseize — the exact disruption combo hates. You can also go the prison route: Eldrazi (3.1%) with Chalice of the Void on 1 shuts off cantrips and rituals. Red Stompy (2.7%) combines Blood Moon, Chalice, and Trinisphere. Death and Taxes (2.0%) uses Thalia to tax all noncreature spells. If you can't beat them, join them — Oops (8.2%) races other combo decks and has its own Force of Will.

instruction: What should I play in a fair-heavy Legacy meta?
output: In a fair-heavy meta, go over the top with card advantage or combo: Sneak and Show (6.7%) ignores fair creature decks entirely by putting Emrakul into play. Lands (3.8%) grinds out any fair deck with Life from the Loam, Wasteland recursion, and the Dark Depths combo. Beanstalk Control (2.3%) generates massive card advantage with Up the Beanstalk off pitch spells. Combo decks like TES (3.5%) and Doomsday (2.7%) are also strong since fair decks pack less combo hate in their sideboards.

instruction: What are Ocelot Pride Midrange's matchups?
output: Ocelot Pride Midrange at 8.5% has a mixed matchup spread. Slightly unfavorable vs Dimir Tempo — Bowmasters and Thoughtseize are effective, but the deck can grind through disruption with token generation. Unfavorable vs combo without blue countermagic — relies on Thalia effects and fast clocks to race. Favorable vs prison/Stompy — goes wide around Chalice of the Void since most of its threats cost more than 1. Even vs Lands — can race the Dark Depths combo with a fast enough board. The deck's biggest weakness is the lack of Force of Will against combo.

instruction: What sideboard cards are most popular in Legacy right now?
output: The most-played sideboard cards reflect the meta's demands: Leyline of the Void is in 36% of decks — graveyard hate is essential with Oops at 8.2% and Reanimator still present. Surgical Extraction sees heavy play for precision combo hate. Pyroblast and Hydroblast are staples in the blue mirror. Flusterstorm covers Storm, counter wars, and combo. Force of Negation provides additional free interaction. Plague Engineer punishes token strategies like Ocelot Pride. Null Rod and Collector Ouphe hit artifact decks. Sideboard construction must balance graveyard hate, anti-blue cards, and anti-combo tools.

instruction: How has Orcish Bowmasters changed the Legacy metagame?
output: Orcish Bowmasters, printed in Lord of the Rings (June 2023), has been format-warping. At 29% of decks, it is the most played creature in Legacy. Its impact: it killed Elves as a competitive deck by pinging mana dorks, weakened Delver mirrors by punishing cantrips, and enabled Dimir Tempo as the top deck by giving black a creature that generates value passively. Cards that draw multiple cards (Brainstorm, Sylvan Library, Uro) became riskier. Small creatures with 1 toughness became worse. The card made black the best partner color for blue tempo shells, overtaking red.

instruction: How is Storm positioned in the current Legacy meta?
output: The EPIC Storm (TES) is at 3.5% meta share, making it a solid tier 2 deck. It uses Burning Wish for flexibility, Lion's Eye Diamond for fast mana, and wins with Tendrils of Agony or Empty the Warrens. In the current meta, Storm faces challenges: Dimir Tempo (14.6%) runs Thoughtseize, Force of Will, and Daze — the exact disruption Storm hates. However, Storm is strong against fair non-blue decks and can race other combo. The deck rewards deep format knowledge and is one of the hardest decks to play well in Legacy.

instruction: Is Reanimator still playable in Legacy after the Entomb ban?
output: Reanimator still exists at about 2.0% meta share but is significantly weaker without Entomb. The deck now relies on Faithful Mending, Careful Study, and other discard outlets to get creatures into the graveyard, which is slower and less consistent than the old turn-1 Entomb into Reanimate line. The deck still plays Griselbrand, Atraxa, Reanimate, and Exhume, but it is now a tier 3 strategy rather than the tier 1 force it was before the ban. Many former Reanimator players have moved to Oops! All Spells or Sneak and Show.

instruction: Is Doomsday a good deck in Legacy?
output: Doomsday is at 2.7% meta share, solidly tier 2-3. It is one of the most skill-intensive decks in any format — you cast Doomsday to stack 5 specific cards as your library, then draw through them to win with Thassa's Oracle. The deck is strong against fair blue mirrors because it can construct piles that play around Force of Will by including its own countermagic. It struggles against fast combo (slower than Oops or Storm) and prison (Chalice shuts off cantrips needed to draw through the pile). If you enjoy complex puzzles and high-skill gameplay, Doomsday is rewarding.

instruction: How is Death and Taxes positioned in the current Legacy meta?
output: Death and Taxes is at 2.0% meta share, tier 3. It uses Thalia Guardian of Thraben to tax noncreature spells, Aether Vial to deploy creatures without paying mana (dodging countermagic), Rishadan Port and Wasteland for mana denial, and Stoneforge Mystic to tutor equipment. The deck is strong against combo (Thalia is devastating) and blue cantrip decks. It struggles against Lands (can't mana-deny a land deck) and against decks that go wide (Ocelot Pride tokens overwhelm one-for-one removal). It recently won the TOPdeck.Ru Championship as an Orzhov variant.

instruction: What is the cheapest competitive Legacy deck?
output: The cheapest competitive Legacy decks are: Oops! All Spells at about $1,100 — a tier 1 combo deck with 8.2% meta share that runs zero dual lands. It wins by milling itself with Balustrade Spy and winning with Thassa's Oracle. Belcher at about $1,200 — another zero-land combo deck. Burn at about $1,500 — straightforward aggro that doesn't need dual lands. All three avoid Reserved List cards entirely, which is what makes Legacy expensive. Oops is particularly notable as a budget tier 1 option.
